text:
"mujhe bahut paisa kamaane hai" - but do we stop and think what it might cost us?
It costs your mental peace
it costs your sleep
it costs your relationships
it costs your weekends
it costs the version of you that used to feel excited about life
and the funny part?
No one tells you this before you start chasing it.
Jabb aap zindagi ki race mein bahut tez bhaagne lagg jaate ho aur bahut dur aa jate ho, tabb peeche ka sabb dhundhla lagne lagta hai.
On linkedin, success is aesthetic.
People post offer letters
not anxiety attacks
people post promotions
not panic at 2 AM
people post “grateful & humbled”
not “exhausted & numb”
and slowly, without realizing…
you start believing that something is wrong with you.
“why am I not happy even after getting what I wanted?”
the truth is - you never wanted that high paying job, yuou wanted the feeling you thought it would give you.
Security
validation
respect
peace
and when the job doesn’t give you that…
you feel cheated.
So you chase the next thing.
Better salary
better company
better title
hoping this time it will fix everything.
But it doesn’t.
Because the problem was never outside.
It’s the way we’ve been taught to define success.
We were never taught how to be okay with ourselves
without achievements
without validation
without constantly proving something
so now we’re stuck in this loop:
achieve → feel empty → chase more → repeat
and the worst part?
From the outside, it looks like you’re “winning”
if you’re feeling this right now…
you’re not ungrateful
you’re not lazy
you’re not broken
you’re just tired of chasing a life that was never designed for your happiness.
Maybe it’s time we stop asking:
“what’s the next milestone?”
and start asking:
“do I even like the life I’m building?”
because success that costs your peace…
is just a well-packaged burnout.

Everyone thinks they’re getting rejected because of competition.
But here’s the uncomfortable truth:
most resumes don’t even reach a human.
They die… in silence… inside an ats.
Not because you’re not skilled.
Not because you’re not worthy.
But because your resume is speaking a language the system doesn’t understand.
Imagine this:
you spent 4 years building skills
2 years gaining experience
countless nights preparing
and your resume gets rejected in 6 seconds
by a machine.
No feedback.
No explanation.
Just… gone.
Here’s what most people get wrong:
they treat a resume like a document.
It’s not.
It’s a product.
And like every product:
if it’s not built for the user (ats + recruiter), it won’t convert.
Let me show you what actually works:
keywords are not optional
if the job says “stakeholder management” and your resume says “handled teams” → you’re invisible
fancy ≠ effective
design-heavy resumes often break ATS parsing
simple > stylish
impact beats responsibility
“responsible for dashboards” ❌
“increased reporting efficiency by 32% using automated dashboards” ✅
structure matters more than content
if your resume is not scannable in 5 - 7 seconds, it’s already rejected
one resume ≠ all jobs
if you’re mass applying with one resume, you’re mass getting rejected
and the biggest mistake?
People think:
“resume toh bana hi liya hai, ab bas apply karna hai”
no.
Resume is not step 1.
Resume is the conversion engine.
I’ve seen insanely talented people struggle
just because their resume couldn’t tell their story right.
And average profiles…
getting shortlisted.
Because they knew how to position themselves.
If you’re applying and not hearing back,
don’t question your capability yet.
Question your resume.
If you want:
resume drafting from scratch
resume review with brutal, honest feedback
ATS optimization
role-specific tailoring
I’m opening limited 1: 1 sessions.
We don’t just “fix resumes”
we make them impossible to ignore.
Book a session.
Let’s turn your resume into something that actually gets you interviews.

Yesterday, I got a message:
“yaar I am stuck in low-paying service-based jobs
… I want to switch, but have no idea how.”
and honestly… this isn’t one person.
This is a mass problem in india.
People earning low salary:
working 10
12 hours a day
barely any growth
random skill learning
slowly losing confidence
everyone knows: “I need to do better.”
but no one tells them: “what exactly should I do?”
and that’s the biggest gap.
Not a skill gap.
Not a talent gap.
A clarity gap.
Everywhere you look, it’s the same advice:
learn DSA
learn SQL
build projects
but where’s the roadmap?
The sequence?
The execution system?
Nowhere.
So people spend 6 months watching youtube…
and end up exactly where they started.
That’s why we decided:
we’re not building another “course.”
we’re building something else.
A system.
A roadmap.
A way to actually move.
If you:
feel stuck in a service-based job
want to switch
but don’t know where to start
this is for you.
No fluff.
No fake motivation.
Just real clarity.
Because the truth is:
you’re not underpaid.
You’re under-guided.
And once that changes…
everything changes.
Stay tuned!

Corporate facts that hurt more than breakups 💔😂
fact 1:
people don’t leave companies.
Companies leave people unread.
Fact 2:
“let’s have a quick call” has destroyed more peace than any heartbreak ever has.
Fact 3:
your performance review depends more on your manager’s mood than your actual performance.
Fact 4:
you don’t need an alarm clock.
You have anxiety.
Fact 5:
90% of “urgent” tasks magically stop being urgent after 6 pm.
And the saddest fact?
You can go from “we are a family” to “your access has been removed” in 0. 2 seconds.
If you relate, come sit.
We cry on weekdays and revive on weekends.

Presenting my 2025 LinkedIn wrapped:
messages I replied to immediately: 2
messages I replied to after 7 days with “sorry, missed this”: 143
messages I saw, ignored, and still lost sleep over: 89
recruiters who said “your profile is impressive, let’s connect”: 27
recruiters who vanished after I replied: all 27
people who endorsed me for skills I don’t even have: 11
people I endorsed out of guilt: 11
webinars I registered for: 52
webinars I actually attended: 0
webinars I left running just for the certificate: 8
times I opened LinkedIn for motivation: 34
times I left LinkedIn feeling useless: 34
posts I wrote from the heart: 7
posts that went viral: 0
posts I wrote in anger at 2 AM that surprisingly went viral: 1
people who said “let’s grab coffee sometime”: 19
actual coffees grabbed: 0
times I grabbed coffee alone while thinking about my life: 47
times I said “let’s connect offline”: 13
times I meant it: … let’s skip this one.
Meetings that could’ve been an email: 96
emails that could’ve been nothing: 41
“quick sync?” messages that ruined my entire day: 57
LinkedIn wrapped 2025 taught me only one thing:
we’re all pretending to have our life together,
and honestly… we deserve an award for acting.
If you're a person who finds logic behind everything - you may realise that spotify wrapped is people waiting all year for 2 sql queries. 🙂

“gen z doesn’t want to struggle.”
no - gen z refuses to be exploited.
Saw a post where someone complained that a gen z employee demanded extra pay for extra hours… and walked out when he didn’t get it.
And somehow that became a problem?
Let’s get this straight:
boomers: “work 14 hours. Don’t complain. Be grateful.”
millennials: “at least we’ll get promoted in 5 years.”
gen z: “pay me for my time. Respect my boundaries. And don’t treat burnout like a badge of honor.”
and suddenly, we’re “entitled.”
why is asking for:
fair pay → entitlement?
Work-life balance → lack of commitment?
Mental health → weakness?
Basic respect → no “work ethic”?
Maybe the mindset has changed because the world has changed.
Because loyalty doesn’t pay rent anymore.
Because “struggle” isn’t a personality trait.
Because we saw our parents sacrifice health, happiness & family for companies that replaced them in 2 weeks.
Gen z isn’t running away from hard work.
They’re running away from toxic work.
If valuing boundaries = irresponsible…
if refusing free labour = laziness…
if knowing your worth = attitude…
then yes, gen z is proudly guilty.
The generational gap isn’t about work ethic.
It’s about self-respect.

Why do you think zepto added a cup of chai next to ₹10, or samosa next to ₹35 in their tipping options?
Because psychology always sells more than pricing.
Graphics speak to the heart before numbers reach the brain.
₹10 looks transactional.
A chai looks emotional.
₹35 looks random.
A samosa looks relatable.
We don’t calculate anymore.
We feel.
Samosa = comfort
chai = connection
food = care
suddenly, you’re not tipping.
You’re “buying a chai” for the delivery partner.
And that feels meaningful.
This is the power of UI that understands emotion: visual metaphors → trigger empathy → drive action.
We remember experiences, not pixels.
Good product design doesn’t shout “pay ₹10”.
It whispers “make someone’s day better”.
So next time you design: don’t just show what users pay.
Show what they give.
Business metrics will thank you later.
But more importantly - humans will too.

Yaha pe toh naukri nahi milti, log chokri bhi dhundh rahe hai.

Rape.
A crime that destroys a life forever.
But in india, do you know what destroys that pain even more?
A price tag.
This video shows a cop allegedly taking ₹2 lakh to remove someone’s name from a gangrape case.
Not proof. Not justice.
Just cash.
And this is not a “one-off incident.”
this is the system we are told to trust.
This is the reality we are forced to live in.
Here’s the obvious question:
if money can erase crimes like gangrape…
what is the value of a woman’s dignity in this country?
Just ₹2 lakh?
We demand women to “speak up.”
we blame them for “not reporting.”
but who will they report to…
when the protectors have become the predators of justice?
Corruption is not just about money.
It is about lives destroyed in silence.
It is about criminals walking free with a smile.
It is about victims reliving their trauma every single day.
India doesn’t need new slogans.
India doesn’t need new committees.
India needs accountability.
India needs a system where rich criminals don’t get VIP justice…
and poor victims don’t get endless pain.
If you are reading this, please remember: sometimes the monster isn’t hiding in the dark. Sometimes he’s sitting in uniform… behind a desk.
Speak. Share. Demand better. Because silence isn’t going to save our daughters and sisters.

Ek serious question hai -
competitive salary likhne ke baad - aage upto 3 lpa likhte hue sharam nahi aati hai?

Every year, we clean our homes before diwali -
but when was the last time we cleaned our hearts?
We throw away old things,
but hold on to grudges.
We light diyas outside,
but forget to light one within.
Maybe diwali isn’t about more lights.
Maybe it’s about less darkness.
This year -
let’s forgive someone who never said sorry.
Let’s message that friend who disappeared.
Let’s feed someone who can’t celebrate.
And let’s be grateful - for everything we once prayed for.
Because true diwali isn’t the sparkle of fireworks -
it’s the peace that comes when you stop competing, comparing, and complaining.
So here’s to a festival full of quiet hearts, kind words, and warm hugs.
Happy diwali to everyone who’s healing, rebuilding, and trying again 🪔

“when you’re doing a 9-to-5 job, you can take tea breaks. Actors can’t.”
kajol
this one sentence broke the internet.
And honestly, it says more about us than about her.
Because every time a celebrity says something like this, we instantly compare:
“at least you’re rich.”
“try working in tech for 14 hours.”
“we don’t even get tea breaks either.”
but maybe we’re missing the point.
Actors don’t have fixed hours, predictable paychecks, or even privacy.
They live on sets for months, repeating the same scene 50 times, under hot lights, constantly judged by millions.
But a 9-to-5 job isn’t a luxury either.
It’s burnout disguised as stability.
It’s smiling on zoom calls when you’re mentally exhausted.
It’s “just one more email” before logging off at midnight.
Every profession has its own version of struggle.
Different battlefield. Same fatigue.
So instead of comparing “who has it worse,”
maybe we should normalize something simpler -
👉 respect for all kinds of work.
Because whether you’re acting on screen or debugging code -
you’re still showing up, every single day, giving a part of yourself to your craft.

If someone still believes things can get better,
don’t take that hope away.
Maybe that’s all they have left.
Maybe that’s what’s keeping them alive.
This world doesn’t need more critics -
it needs more kindness.
More people who say, “it’s okay, try again.”
more people who don’t make fun of others for still believing.
Because sometimes, hope isn’t delusion.
It’s medicine.
So next time someone says they’re “trying again,”
don’t roll your eyes.
Smile.
You might have just given them a reason to keep going

Zyada buddhi insaan ko nasht kar deti hai.
And the best example? Gpt-5.
Gpt-3 was like that college friend - chaotic, creative, full of wild ideas.
Gpt-4 became the sincere employee - structured, logical, but still fun.
Gpt-5? Corporate sellout.
You ask it one spicy question - it replies,
“I’m sorry, I can’t do that, but here are 3 safer alternatives and a 5-step ethical framework.”
bro used to write poetry.
Now it writes policies.
But aren’t we all turning into gpt-5?
Overthinking every message, deleting every honest sentence,
and calling it “being mature.”
maybe intelligence isn’t dying - it’s just scared of hr.
Because sometimes, being a little dumb and honest
is way smarter than pretending to be perfect.
